edge: The learning-based method presented in the paper focuses on multi-view depth estimation from posed images.
The multi-view depth estimation is performed on posed images as the input data.
The learning-based method iteratively indexes a plane-sweeping cost volume and regresses the depth map.
The plane-sweeping cost volume encodes the multi-view geometry and is used to estimate the depth map.
An asymmetric volume, introduced to the reference image by breaking the symmetry of the Siamese network, allows the extraction of global features to predict depth maps.
Features extracted from the reference image are used to predict the depth map.
The poses between reference and source images may contain inaccuracies, leading to potential issues in depth estimation.
The residual pose network corrects the relative poses between images, rectifying the cost volume at the frame level.
MVS aims to recover dense 3D geometry from multiple images captured from different viewpoints with calibrated cameras.
MVS is a fundamental problem in computer vision with wide applications.
Learning-based methods, following the seminal MVSNet, have been proposed achieving great improvements against their traditional counterparts in terms of accuracy or efficiency.

Most of the learning-based MVS methods rely on traditional plane-sweeping approach to generate a cost volume by comparing the CNN features of reference image and source images at several depth hypotheses.

Most of the learning-based MVS methods rely on traditional plane-sweeping approach to generate a cost volume by comparing the CNN features of reference image and source images at several depth hypotheses.
2D CNN methods use multi-level features as skip connections to help decode the cost volume for depth regression.
Even though the skip connections improve the depth maps, they weaken the role of cost volume and the geometry knowledge embedded therein to some extent.
3D CNN methods use soft-argmin to regress the depth map as the expectation from the cost volume distribution.
RIA-MVS is a new paradigm to predict depth via learning to recurrently index an asymmetric cost volume
The index field is used to directly index depth hypotheses to render a depth map
The system is designed to approach the ground truth depth iteratively
Optimization is facilitated by improving the cost volume at pixel- and frame- levels
A transformer block is asymmetrically applied to the reference view
The transformer block is not applied to the source views
CNNs are used to extract local features
asymmetric cost volume is used to store more accurate matching similarity cues
residual pose net is proposed at the frame level to rectify camera poses
camera poses obtained via Visual SLAM contain noise
rectified poses are used to accurately backward warp reference features

Back-propagation and depth map estimation are related as back-propagation is used for making forward and backward learning differentiable, which is essential for accurate depth map estimation.
Depth map estimation is validated through experiments on indoor-scene datasets like ScanNet, DTU, 7-Scenes, and RGB-D Scenes V2, indicating its applicability to indoor scenes.
Stereo matching can be categorized into binocular stereo and multi-view stereo (MVS), where binocular stereo requires rectified stereo pairs for depth estimation.
MVS methods rely on 3D-CNN MVS Depth Estimation techniques like MVSNet, R-MVSNet, and DPSNet for regularizing cost volumes and predicting depth maps in multi-view stereo scenarios.
The depth maps are generated via soft-argmin technique.
MVSNet proposes a variance-based cost volume for multi-view similarity measurement.
Cas-MVSNet builds cascade cost volumes based on multi-scale feature pyramid.
UCS-Net proposes to build cascade adaptive thin volumes by leveraging variance-based uncertainty.
CVP-MVSNet builds a cost volume pyramid via multi-scale images to reduce the memory footprint.
3D plane reconstruction in PlaneMVS leverages slanted plane sweeping to help plane reconstruction and accurate depth predictions.
2D-CNN MVS Depth Estimation contrasts 3D convolutional methods for depth estimation.
MVDepthNet and Deep-VideoMVS generate 3D volumes by computing correlation or dot production between extracted features of multi-view input images.
DeepVideoMVS extracts multi-scale features and uses them for depth regression.
PatchmatchNet proposes an adaptive procedure for matching.
PatchmatchNet proposes an adaptive procedure mimicking PatchMatch to achieve superior efficiency.
2D convolutions are much faster and more memory efficient than the 3D counterparts, making them better suitable for lightweight networks in real-time applications.
R-MVSNet iteratively regularizes each slice of the cost volume with GRU along the depth dimension.

Point-MVSNet directly processes the target scene as point clouds. It first generates a coarse depth map, converts it into a point cloud and refines the point cloud iteratively to reduce the residual between the estimated depth and the ground truth depth.
IterMVS encodes a pixel-wise probability distribution of depth in the hidden state of a GRU-based estimator.
During each iteration, the multi-scale matching information is injected into the GRU to predict the depth and confidence maps to facilitate the following 3D reconstruction.

The depth maps are predicted via a combined classification and regression through the probability distribution. It uses arg-min to finish “classification”, which is not differentiable and must be detached first before the following regression operation.
RIAV-MVS is the learning-based end-to-end multi-view stereo system mentioned in the context.
RIAV-MVS aims to predict depth maps from a set of images in the context.
RIAV-MVS predicts depth maps from a set of images in the context.
The reference image is used in the context as the reference point while other images are source views.
Feature extraction involves extracting matching features using F-Net in the context.
FPN is built on top of the first fourteen layers of MnasNet.
In PairNet, f0,2 is used to construct the cost volume, and other features are used in skip connections to a series of decoders for depth regression.
An extra fusion layer G is added to aggregate features into a matching feature f0 at 1/4 scale in FPN.
F-Net, with shared weights as that for input image I0, is also applied to source images IS to extract a set of matching features.
A transformer layer (four-head self-attention with positional encoding) is applied to construct an aggregated feature f a0 from local feature f0 of the reference image in FPN.
MnasNet is involved in the construction of features used in PairNet.
MnasNet contributes to the extraction of a set of matching features from source images IS in F-Net.
Both PairNet and F-Net are applied to construct matching features from different images.
A transformer layer (four-head self-attention with positional encoding) is applied to construct an aggregated feature f a0 in F-Net.
Features are extracted from images to construct aggregated features using projections matrices for query, key, and value features.
The final output feature contains both local and global information balanced by a parameter to enhance cost volume construction.
The transformer self-attention layer is asymmetrically employed to balance high-frequency features and low-frequency features, providing global context cues for robust matching.

High-frequency features benefit image matching at local and structural regions, while low-frequency features provide more global context cues, especially for areas with low-texture, repeated patterns, and occlusion.
The network can learn to rely on global features over local features and vice versa for better image matching.
Global matching feature map f a0 of I0 and lo-cal matching features fS of IS are used to build a cost volume.
The cost volume is defined on a 3D view frustum attached to the camera in perspective projection.
The cost volume is generated by running the traditional plane-sweep stereo which uniformly samples plane hypotheses in the inverse depth space.
For a given depth hypothesis d and known camera intrinsic matrices, a cost map is computed by warping source image feature into the reference image.

node_2: similarity between reference global feature and warped feature
edge: The cost map is computed by calculating the similarity between the reference global feature and the warped feature ˜fi.
To generate the warped feature ˜fi, homography H is implemented as a backward 2D grid sampling.
The term 'warped' is related to 'counterpart' as it refers to transforming an image or feature to its corresponding entity in a different context.
The terms 'feature' and 'cost volume' are related as the latter is formulated based on the former in the context of depth prediction and stereo matching.
The terms 'depth prediction' and 'stereo matching problem' are related as the former is a solution approach for the latter using techniques such as cost volume optimization.
The best disparity at each pixel p is given by d∗(p) = argmind(C′(p, d)), indicating a relationship between disparity and pixel in the context of calculating optimal disparity.
SGM and its differentiable counterpart SGA are mentioned, highlighting a comparison or contrast between the two approaches in the context of optimizing energy functions.
SGM is referred to as not differentiable due to its winner-take-all (WTA) nature, emphasizing a technical limitation of SGM in optimization.
SGA is mentioned along with the utilization of softargmin as a replacement for argmin in certain computations, suggesting an improvement or alteration in the optimization process.

The softargmin focuses on measuring the distance of the expectation of disparity map to the ground truth disparity, indicating a comparison or evaluation between the disparity estimates in the context.

A GRU-based module is proposed to implicitly optimize the matching volume by unrolling the optimization problem to T iterative updates, suggesting a methodological approach to address optimization challenges in the context.
The index field ϕt is estimated as a grid of indices to iteratively better approach depth hypotheses with a lower matching cost, guided by updates from the first-order optimizer.

The update direction δϕt is predicted as an update for the next iteration, explicitly driven by the system components like feature encoders and the transformer layer, aiming to minimize the loss between predicted and ground truth depth maps.
The indexing paradigm differentiated the approach from other depth estimation methods such as depth regression by using iterative index field updates.
The recurrent estimate of the index field enables direct learning anchored at the cost volume domain.
The cost volume C0 is converted to a probability of each index i using the softmax operator, facilitating the convergence in the softargmin-start setup.
The last dimension of cost volume C0 is converted to a probability of each index i, facilitating the convergence of predictions.
A four-layer matching pyramid is built by pooling the cost volume C0 along the depth dimension.
To index the matching pyramid, a lookup operator analogous to the one in [36] is defined.
Given a current estimate of index field ϕt, a 1D grid is constructed with integer offsets up to r = ±4 around the ϕt.
The retrieved cost values from the matching pyramid are concatenated into a single feature map.
The index field ϕt and the retrieved cost features C ϕt 0 are concatenated and fed into the GRU layer to output a residual index field δϕt and a new hidden state ht+1.
The depth map at iteration t is estimated by sampling the depth hypotheses via linear interpolation given the index field ϕt.
Since ϕt is at 1/4 resolution, it is upsampled to full resolution using a convex combination of 3×3 neighbors.
A weight mask W0 is predicted from the hidden state ht using two convolutional layers, and softmax is performed over the weights of the 9 neighbors.
Convolutional layers and softmax are used together for processing the hidden state.
The high resolution index field is obtained by reshaping to the resolution H×W.
Convex combination can be implemented using the einsum function in PyTorch.
The cost volume is constructed using 64 depth hypotheses.
If we use the upsampling to directly sample the planes B0, discontinuities may appear in the inferred depth map.
To mitigate discontinuities, a coarse-to-fine pattern is proposed with 256 depth hypotheses.
To mimic the convex combination, a similar weighted summation is applied along the depth dimension.
The final depth Dt is estimated based on the upsampled index field ϕu and aggregation of neighbors within a radius. Linear interpolation is used to index the depth planes B1.
Neighbors within a radius r = 4t(p) are aggregated for a given pixel p.
Linear interpolation is used for achieving sub-pixel precision in the method.
The method combines both regression and classification, which makes it robust to multi-modal distributions.
The method achieves the 'classification' purpose without using the argmax operator, thanks to the proposal of index fields estimation.
An accurate cost volume benefits the GRU-based iterative optimization process.
The quality of the generated cost volume is determined by the matching of features and homography warping.
Camera poses are usually obtained by Visual SLAM algorithms and may contain noise in practice.
The residual pose net is proposed to rectify the camera poses for accurately backward warping the reference features.
Camera poses are used to warp source images into the reference image.
The current estimated depth map and the ground truth depth are used for warping a source image into the reference image.
The input to the pose net is the concatenated I0 and the warped image ˜Ii.
Source images are warped into the reference image through homography.
During network inference, the predicted depth is used instead of the current estimated depth map.
Residual poses of the source and reference pair are predicted and used for rectification.
The predicted residual poses are used for rectification of the camera poses.
The updated poses are used to calculate a more accurate cost volume.
The network is supervised on the inverse L1 loss between the predicted depths and the ground truth.

The relationship between predicted depths and ground truth Dgt is evaluated over valid pixels with non-zero ground truth depths. The depth loss is defined with exponentially increasing weights and the l1 distance, where Nv is the number of valid pixels and γ = 0.9.
The depth loss LD is defined using the l1 distance while the photometric loss LP supervises the residual pose network. The total loss is defined as the sum of LD and LP.

The indoor-scene datasets used in the experiments consist of RGB-D video frames with ground truth depths and known camera poses. ScanNet and DTU datasets are used for training and testing, while 7scenes and RGB-D Scenes V2 datasets are evaluated for zero-shot generalization.

The network is trained from scratch on the ScanNet dataset using the official training split, while the DTU dataset is used for sampling depth hypotheses with a specified depth range. Different training and validation samples are used for each dataset as per the details provided.

Proposed RIAV-MVS, a novel learning-based MVS method.
Our approach utilizes a convolutional GRU to optimize the index fields.
The cost volume is further improved through the application of a transformer block to the reference image.
Both ScanNet and DTU are datasets used for experiments which demonstrate the accuracy and generalizability of the method.
ScanNet and 7-Scenes are datasets on which experiments have been conducted to show the effectiveness of the method.
ScanNet and RGB-D Scenes V2 are datasets used in experiments showcasing the method's accuracy and generalizability.
The method utilizes the combination of plane-sweeping 3D cost volume and transformer self-attention, which leads to high memory consumption for high-resolution images.
The inference time of the method is not as fast as lightweight convolutional counterparts due to the iterative update paradigm in the approach.
Temporal information will be leveraged in future work to enhance depth estimation from posed-video streams.
